name: Camas Creek
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding along Camas Creek produces quality agate and jasper specimens. Personal use collecting with no permit required - ideal for Idaho rockhounders seeking accessible digging near J...
description:
Camas Creek in Jefferson County sits within Idaho's productive agate and jasper bearing region. This BLM-managed area offers recreational rockhounders the chance to find colorful chalcedony specimens, banded agates, and jasper in creek beds and surrounding exposures. The creek itself has been a known source for quality material for decades, with persistent collectors reporting good finds in gravel bars and eroded slopes adjacent to the water.

The geology here reflects the Miocene-age volcanic activity that swept across central Idaho, depositing silica-rich fluids and ash that consolidated into agate-bearing rhyolite and chalcedony-filled fractures. Weathering and erosion over millions of years have freed these specimens into Camas Creek's drainage, concentrating material in accessible wash zones.

Expect to work creek beds and nearby hillsides where erosion has exposed colorful specimens. Bring shovels and screens for efficient searching - much of the best material is buried in gravel and sandy soil. This is a moderate-effort site that rewards patient digging and screening.

As BLM land, Camas Creek is free to access with no commercial collection allowed. The 25-pound daily limit per person applies. This makes it a genuine budget-friendly destination for serious collectors and families looking to add Idaho specimens to their collections without fees.
history: Camas Creek sits within Idaho's Miocene volcanic belt, where 20-25 million years ago silica-rich hydrothermal fluids percolated through fractured rhyolite and ash deposits, slowly precipitating layers of agate, chalcedony, and jasper. Regional prospecting throughout the 20th century identified multiple productive creek drainages in Jefferson County, including Camas Creek, establishing it as a reliable source for quality material that continues to produce finds today.
tips: Work the active creek bed and gravel bars after seasonal runoff when new material is exposed - look for translucent and banded specimens eroding from the banks. Bring a fine screen and shovel to process creek gravels efficiently; many best specimens are thumb-sized and easy to miss without screening. Hunt the base of cut banks and exposed slope faces where weather has loosened specimens - Camas Creek's south-facing slopes often produce the most colorful agate. Early morning light helps spot the glassy luster of chalcedony specimens against darker host rock.
terrain_notes: Camas Creek valley has moderate elevation around 5,500-6,000 feet with open sagebrush and scattered juniper. The creek bed itself is accessible via short walks from parking areas, typically 0.25 to 0.5 miles cross-country. Terrain is rolling to gently sloping with some loose volcanic scree and stable gravels. Footing is generally safe but uneven in places; sturdy boots recommended for creek walking.
best_season: May - October. Late spring through early fall offers best weather for extended digging. Summer can bring afternoon thunderstorms; late season (September-October) often provides clearer skies and cooler temperatures ideal for physical rockhounding work.
written_directions: From Riggins, Idaho (nearest major town), take Idaho Highway 82 south toward Grangeville for approximately 35 miles. Camas Creek drainage is accessible via BLM roads branching east from Highway 82 in the Jefferson County vicinity. Look for BLM access signs near the creek crossing. Consult current BLM maps for exact road numbers and seasonal closures, as some spur roads may require high-clearance vehicles or be impassable in wet conditions.
road_conditions: Variable. Main access via paved state highway; secondary BLM roads range from well-maintained gravel to rough dirt with potential washboard. High-clearance recommended for some spur roads.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas near creek access points. Limited developed facilities. Capacity varies; expect to find space during off-peak seasons.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. All rockhounding must be hand tools only. Respect private property boundaries; stay on BLM land. Pack out all trash.
rules:

nearest_services: Riggins, Idaho approximately 35 miles north - gas, food, lodging. Grangeville approximately 40 miles south - full services.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with gentle slopes and uneven creek bed footing. No wheelchair access. Short walking distances (under 0.5 miles) to main collecting areas. Sturdy boots essential. Not suitable for limited mobility, but achievable for most able-bodied rockhounders with reasonable fitness.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Camas Creek in Idaho?
faq.answer: Camas Creek is best known for producing quality agate, chalcedony, and jasper specimens. You'll find banded agates in a variety of colors, translucent chalcedony, and colorful jasper in creek beds and exposed slope faces. Most specimens are thumb-sized to fist-sized, making them excellent for collectors of all levels.

faq.question: Is Camas Creek free rockhounding or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Camas Creek is free rockhounding on BLM land with no permit required. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day per person for personal use at no cost, making it an ideal budget-friendly destination compared to fee dig sites.

faq.question: What tools do I need to rockhound successfully at Camas Creek?
faq.answer: Bring a shovel and fine-mesh screen for processing creek gravel - this is the most efficient way to find specimens here. A hand pick, small pry bar, and durable work gloves are also helpful. Hand tools only are allowed; no motorized equipment permitted on BLM land.

faq.question: When is the best time of year to rockhound at Camas Creek?
faq.answer: May through October offers the best rockhounding weather. Late spring (May-June) and early fall (September-October) provide ideal conditions - warm days, lower thunderstorm risk, and cooler temperatures for physical digging work. Summer can bring afternoon storms.

faq.question: Is Camas Creek rockhounding good for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Camas Creek is beginner-friendly. The terrain is moderate with short walks to productive areas, and the creek bed material is easy to work. Kids aged 8 and up can participate effectively with supervision. The free access and no-permit requirement make it perfect for families learning Idaho rockhounding.

faq.question: How far is Camas Creek from the nearest town and what services are available?
faq.answer: Camas Creek is approximately 35 miles south of Riggins, Idaho, which has gas stations, restaurants, and lodging. Grangeville is about 40 miles away with more extensive services. Plan for a full day trip and bring plenty of water, as services near the rockhounding area are limited.

meta_title: Camas Creek Rockhounding - Free Idaho Agate Hunting
